messages.text: putting this here for future reference: I can now reliably reconnect a device via bluetooth on the pi, but the steps are super jank. delete device from the pi desktop, bluetoothctl remove doesn't work reboot Heltec V3, remember the new PIN run bluttoothctl, scan on, then pair using mac address after successful pair, turn off and on the heltec v3 when it is back on, script using bluetooth should be able to send messages
messages.content_html:
putting this here for future reference: I can now reliably reconnect a device via bluetooth on the pi, but the steps are super jank.
  • delete device from the pi desktop, bluetoothctl remove doesn't work
  • reboot Heltec V3, remember the new PIN
  • run bluttoothctl, scan on, then pair using mac address
  • after successful pair, turn off and on the heltec v3
  • when it is back on, script using bluetooth should be able to send messages

messages.author: taedryn
messages.text: i'm running into a problem using meshcore_py, where i have a BBS which seems to be responding too quickly to incoming packets. the setup i have is using a BLE companion to connect to the BBS, which is running a USB companion hooked up to a computer running the BBS code. when i send a command to the BBS, it replies instantaneously, before my phone client even thinks its message has been ack'd. then the BBS sends the same thing a couple more times, because it times out waiting for the ack from the client. i suspect that the initial command response is overriding the ack response from the BBS's node. is there any way to see, in the python library, the current state of a sent message, in terms of whether the ack has been sent? is this a situation where it makes more sense to just sleep for a bit before leaping into action?

messages.text: ooh, expected_timeout is good to know about. i need to dig into this further. i was able to solve my too-quick response by brute-forcing a 500 ms delay before responding to a command, but that certainly doesn't scale if the connection is indirect. i'm using send_msg_with_retry , and that will be very helpful as it's likely the bbs will be launching messages to several different people at once during peak usage.

messages.author: pescheck-bram
messages.text: While working on meshcore-cli I noted a few optional ideas and wanted to check first if any of these would be welcome. Please feel free to ignore if not. • Lightweight CI using ruff (lint and formatting only). Very fast and low noise, mainly to keep contributions consistent as the CLI grows. And possible to implement in actions like an other open source project of mine https://github.com/pescheckit/python-gpt-po • Optional Docker image for reproducible CLI runs or testing or user use. Could be handy for quick testing or avoiding local environment issues • Possibly splitting some of the Python code into smaller modules and making parts a bit more generic. Mainly to improve readability and make future changes easier, but only if that aligns with how you want to structure the project. • I also noticed there are argument parsing and autocomplete libraries available that could potentially simplify some CLI-related code. • Testing suite If any of these sound useful, I’m happy to prep small, incremental PRs so you can decide per item. (edited)

messages.author: Just Stuff™
messages.text: I use this to discover repeaters in the Web app. mesh-utility.replit.app function buildNodeDiscoverReq(filter: number): Uint8Array { const tag = Math.floor(Math.random() * 0xFFFFFFFF); const data = new Uint8Array(1 + 1 + 1 + 4); data[0] = CMD_SEND_CONTROL_DATA; data[1] = CONTROL_NODE_DISCOVER_REQ | 0x01; data[2] = filter; data[3] = tag & 0xFF; data[4] = (tag >> 8) & 0xFF; data[5] = (tag >> 16) & 0xFF; data[6] = (tag >> 24) & 0xFF; remoteLog("log", `node_discover cmd: ${Array.from(data).map(b => b.toString(16).padStart(2, "0")).join(" ")}, filter=${filter}, tag=${tag}`); return data; } function parseControlDataFrame(frame: Uint8Array): NodeDiscoverEntry | null { if (frame.length < 5 || frame[0] !== PUSH_CODE_CONTROL_DATA) return null; const view = new DataView(frame.buffer, frame.byteOffset, frame.byteLength); const snrRaw = view.getInt8(1); const snr = snrRaw / 4; const rssi = view.getInt8(2); const pathLen = frame[3]; const payload = frame.slice(4); remoteLog("log", `ControlData raw: snr=${snr} rssi=${rssi} pathLen=${pathLen} payload(${payload.length}b)=${Array.from(payload).map(b => b.toString(16).padStart(2, "0")).join(" ")}`); if (payload.length < 1) { remoteLog("log", `ControlData: empty payload`); return null; } (edited)

I just realized that with pip and pipx you can install directly from a git repo !
  • to install latest meshcore-cli from git : pipx install --force git+https://github.com/meshcore-dev/meshcore-cli.git
  • to install latest meshcore_py in your own environment : pip install --upgrade git+https://github.com/meshcore-dev/meshcore_py.git
  • to install latest meshcore_py in your meshcore-cli pipx install : pipx runpip meshcore-cli install --upgrade git+https://github.com/meshcore-dev/meshcore_py.git
Knowing that I'll probably release less often but more polished revisions to pypi

messages.text: Hello I'm running an app using meshcore-cli to fetch messages from an USB client (Heltec v3). messages.author: accarda
messages.text: Actually after looking at this little bit more, I guess the main issue is that the app keeps running commands that trigger the serial port to execute, so the device turns the OLED on with each interaction. I guess there is not much to do when taking this approach; probably a better approach would be to keep the serial communication opened and then send all commands in that session. So then the OLED should not trigger each time, as the serial is already open. I will look into it may be trying the interactive mode to test this approach. Update: using the interactive mode, I can issue the $sync_msgs to fetch messages and OLED stays off, as the session is already opened. I tried issuing the scope command to send channel msgs with scopes and it works fine now that I have updated to meshcore_py 2.3.5 and meshcore_cli 1.5.5. So this seems to me a viable solution to avoid that the OLED turns on each time. Now it stays off after entering the interactive mode. (edited)
